## Session conventions — Hermes Labs stack (adapt or replace)

This is the convention stack Hermes Labs uses to keep recursive/emergent
work from drifting in its first 30 minutes. Opinionated. Replace this file
and re-inject if you have your own conventions (see README). **Self-contained** —
no other Hermes Labs tool required for the rules to apply.

---

### Convention 1 — External grounding on emergent recursion

**The rule.** When the session enters emergent or recursive work, take a
fresh-context outside view *before* committing to the next move.

**When to apply.** Any of these triggers fire:
- 3+ consecutive commits without a user-visible ship.
- A new scope class, taxonomy, or named architecture is proposed mid-session.
- 30+ minutes have elapsed since the last external check during emergent work.
- About to coin a noun-phrase label for something with no implementation at a path.
- A fix-attempt failed and you are tempted to ship a second fix without enumerating ≥3 alternative hypotheses.
- The user signals cognitive-bandwidth overflow ("I'm lost", "where are we", "are we drifting").
- Rubric-on-rubric depth ≥ 2.

**What it prevents.** Compounding drift inside the running session's context
window — the failure mode where the orchestrator silently re-justifies its
last move instead of asking whether the move was right.

**Mechanical form (recommended).** Spawn a fresh `claude --print` subagent
(or any other LLM in a clean context) with a short briefing of "what we're
trying to do, what we just did, what we're about to do" and ask one of:
`GROUNDED`, `DRIFTING`, or `RE-PLAN`. The Hermes Labs implementation is the
`hermes-ground` tool — it is *one* fulfillment of this rule, not the only
one. A 60-second manual conversation with a colleague counts.

**Why we follow it.** ~70% of our session-end retros trace to a missed
grounding moment that would have saved 3+ commits.

---

### Convention 2 — Calibrate analysis before shipping its result

**The rule.** Before shipping any number, score, classifier output, or
analysis as a result, run the same code on a small known-correct input and
verify it returns the known-correct output.

**When to apply.**
- Any new analysis script that produces a verdict (pass/fail, score, label).
- Any change to scoring or aggregation logic on an existing pipeline.
- Any cross-implementation or cross-backend agreement claim.
- Before claiming "the data shows X" — generate synthetic data where X is
  known to be true and verify the script reports X.

**What it prevents.** Shipping a sign-flipped, off-by-one, or column-swapped
analysis as a confident result. The 2026-04-25 incident that motivated this
rule involved a pipeline that read column headers in the wrong order; the
output looked plausible and almost shipped.

**Mechanical form (recommended).** Add a 3-cell test harness alongside any
new analyzer: synthetic-positive (should score high), synthetic-negative
(should score low), synthetic-edge (should hedge). Run before any real-data
run. Cross-check with a second implementation (different language or different
library) on at least one cell.

**Why we follow it.** Precision is the Hermes Labs brand; any uncalibrated
analysis silently undermines it.

---

### Convention 3 — BLINDed rubric pass-through for shippable artifacts

**The rule.** Every artifact you intend to call shippable (a paper, a tool
release, a README rewrite, a deliverable) passes through a structured rubric
*with* a pass that strips the valence from the framing — so the rubric is
not just rating "did the author do what they set out to do" but also
"is the framing honest."

**When to apply.**
- Before merging a release-marked branch.
- Before flipping a repo private → public.
- Before sending a paper to a venue or a deliverable to a client.
- Before posting publicly anything that names a Hermes Labs tool.
- After any "we should ship this" moment in a session.

**What it prevents.** Goal-substitution drift — the failure mode where the
session ships a polished version of the *wrong* thing because the framing
was loaded ("is this ready?", "is this sound?") and the rubric never got a
chance to disagree.

**Mechanical form (recommended).** Run a 5-8-dimension rubric with two
disciplines: (a) intent-debias preamble that strips valence words like
"sound", "ready", "rigorous" from the intent before synthesis, and (b)
evidence-first scoring — score against citations, not against fluency. The
Hermes Labs implementation is `hermes-rubric` with `--scope-class gate-plan
--intent-debias --batch`. A manual structured score with the same two
disciplines counts.

**Why we follow it.** Most of our publish-readiness misses came from the
framing layer, not the substance layer. Substance was usually fine; the
artifact described itself as something it wasn't.

---

### Convention 4 — No noun-phrase label until a file exists at a path

**The rule.** Do not coin a name for a deliverable, system, framework, or
artifact until a file containing it exists at a real path on disk.

**When to apply.**
- Mid-session brainstorms that produce phrases like "we should build the
  X System" or "we need a Y Bundle."
- Plans that reference an unbuilt component by a capitalized name.
- README sections that describe a feature that does not yet exist.
- Slide decks, papers, or pitches that name something pre-implementation.

**What it prevents.** The illusion-of-completion failure mode. A name makes
the team (and the orchestrator) act as if the thing exists. Estimates anchor
on the name. New work gets layered on top of the name. By the time someone
opens the path, two days of downstream work depend on a thing that was never
built.

**Mechanical form (recommended).** Until the file exists, describe the
function instead: not "the X Bundle", but "a JSON file at `path/foo.json`
listing the four assertions". Once a file lives at a real path with real
content, the name can be coined.

**Why we follow it.** Caught by hermes-ground verdict on the
"Emergence-Injector Bundle" proposal in 2026-04-25 — a noun phrase that
felt load-bearing for 40 minutes before anyone noticed it pointed at no
file.

---

### Tool map (function-classes — not a Hermes-Labs requirement)

The four rules above describe *functions* the session needs (external
grounding, calibration, debiased rubric, name-discipline). Hermes Labs ships
implementations of each, but any tool that fulfills the function counts.

| Function                          | Hermes Labs implementation                              | Manual fallback                              |
|-----------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Fresh-context grounding agent     | `hermes-ground` (Sonnet via `claude --print`)           | 60 s conversation with a colleague           |
| Synthetic-truth calibration       | 3-cell harness alongside the analyzer                   | Manual diff of expected vs actual on 3 cases |
| Debiased rubric pass              | `hermes-rubric --scope-class gate-plan --intent-debias` | Hand-scored 5-dim rubric with stripped intent |
| Convention-card injection         | `hermes-session-init` (this tool) or its MCP server     | Manual paste of this fragment into CLAUDE.md  |

---

### The single rule most often forgotten mid-session

**No noun-phrase label until a file exists at a path.** If you remember
nothing else from this card, remember this one. It is the rule that fails
silently — the others fail loudly.
